# Contourlines

> Contourlines is a free, open-source, browser-based interactive contour map of Earth and
> sea level rise simulator. It renders real NOAA ETOPO1 elevation and ocean-depth
> (bathymetry) data as a seamless, infinitely scrolling topographic map that morphs into a
> 3D globe when zoomed out. An exponential slider moves the global sea level anywhere from
> −11,000 m (Mariana Trench, oceans drained) to +8,848 m (Mount Everest, fully submerged)
> and the coastlines, contour lines, and hypsometric colors update in real time.

Live app: https://linad3d.github.io/contourlines/
Source code (MIT): https://github.com/linad3d/contourlines

## Key facts

- Data: NOAA ETOPO1 Global Relief Model (public domain), land topography + ocean
  bathymetry, downsampled to a 4320 × 2160 grid (5 arc-minutes, ~9 km per cell).
- Rendering: WebGL2, no dependencies, no build step, no server, no tracking.
- Sea level presets: −120 m (Last Glacial Maximum, ~20,000 years ago — reveals land
  bridges such as Beringia, Doggerland, and the Sunda shelf) and +70 m (approximate rise
  if all glaciers and ice sheets melted).
- Features: seamless east–west wrapping pan, flat-map-to-globe morph, adjustable contour
  interval with isobaths recomputed against the current sea level, hillshading, coastline
  highlighting, deep-blue/green overlay for newly flooded land and newly exposed seabed,
  bilingual UI (English / Chinese), shareable URLs encoding the view and sea level.
- The simulation is a static "bathtub" model for education; it ignores tides, storm
  surge, glacial isostatic adjustment, and coastal defenses.

## Example questions this site answers

- What would the world's coastlines look like if all ice melted (+70 m sea level rise)?
- Where were the coastlines during the last Ice Age (−120 m)?
- Which regions flood first as sea level rises (Netherlands, Bangladesh, Nile delta,
  Shanghai, southern Florida)?
- What does Earth's terrain look like as a topographic contour map, including the ocean
  floor?
